content:
I plan to write a driver for a virtio-vhost-user device, which allows VMs to implement vhost-user devices. A draft Cloud Hypervisor PR is at https://github.com/cloud-hypervisor/cloud-hypervisor/pull/7695.

How hard would it be to add safe Rust bindings for the existing virtio code?

Demi Obenour said:

How hard would it be to add safe Rust bindings for the existing virtio code?

28 i guess.
How would I get started?
I'm not a virtio super user, but I thought that guests still would use the same virtio drivers when they are talking to qemu virtio devices and virtio-vhost-user devices?
I'm imlementing the driver for the virtio-vhost-user device. You are correct that guests using the device implemented by the virtio-vhost-user device would use the original driver.

I should come up with standard terms for this distinction, such as “proxy device” (the virtio-vhost-user device itself) vs “implemented device” (the device that the virtio-vhost-user device is used to implement).
Ah. But the virtio-vhost-user implementation lives in user space, right? (sorry if it is a stupid question).
I’m writing a driver that will allow userspace programs to use the virtio-vhost-user device to implement vhost-user devices for use by other guests.
No, it’s not a stupid question.
Yes, you can implement a virtio-vhost-user device using virtio-vhost-user.
Where in this diagram does the driver you write live?
Guest kernel

It’s a driver for a novel virtio device that requires access to one or two additional PCI capabilities normal virtio devices don’t expose. It also requires additional MSI-X interrupts beyond what normal virtio devices provide.

Ok, cool.

Virtio guest devices can be both PCI and platform devices, right?
I guess you could write a new PCI driver. We have a bit of PCI stuff exposed in safe Rust APIs.
You could also see if you can wrap the virtio PCI drivers in Rust APIs.

The data flow is:

Location Software Protocol used to talk to next stage
Backend guest userspace Vhost-user device implementation Ioctls or io_uring
Backend guest kernel Virtio-vhost-user device driver Virtio-vhost-user over PCI
Cloud Hypervisor Virtio-vhost-user device model Vhost-user over AF_UNIX
Cloud Hypervisor Generic vhost-user device Virtio over PCI
Frontend guest kernel Driver for specific virtio device N/A

Andreas Hindborg said:

You could also see if you can wrap the virtio PCI drivers in Rust APIs.

That was my thought, but I would need to extend those devices to get extra MSI interrupts.

Demi Obenour said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

You could also see if you can wrap the virtio PCI drivers in Rust APIs.

That was my thought, but I would need to extend those devices to get extra MSI interrupts.

Right. You could extend the C driver framework (in C) with the additional features, and the expose those to Rust as well.
Then you can write your driver in Rust using those APIs.
Are most APIs fairly straightforward to wrap, or are they going to have nasty problems wrapping safely?

Demi Obenour said:

Are most APIs fairly straightforward to wrap, or are they going to have nasty problems wrapping safely?

That question is difficult to answer. Some stuff is easy, some stuff requires more thought. Some times when we build an API with a specific use in mind, they do not work for other users. It can be tricky to figure out how to design APIs so that they are safe, intuitive to the user, and cover all users. Often we don't get it right in first try. It is mostly an iterative process.

But some things are more straight forward.

There are plenty of Rust APIs already, so there is plenty of inspiration. Maybe you should take a look at the Rust PCI APIs, interrupt APIs, IO memory, DMA, Driver API, etc. If you get an understanding of those, I would say you are in a good place to build abstractions on top of C virtio.
Virtio is mostly ring buffers and interrupts, right? I think the GPU people are working with the same concepts, so you could take a look there as well.
(deleted)
(deleted)
